\section{Introduction}

Actor models provide a foundational framework for concurrent and distributed
computation. In these models, actors are independent computational units that
communicate exclusively via asynchronous message passing. The reception of
messages is handled by a \emph{mailbox}, a dedicated buffer that stores incoming
messages until the actor is ready to process
them~\cite{Hewitt1973,Agha1986,foundation97agha}.

In most formalisations of the actor model, the mailbox is treated either as an
opaque runtime primitive or as a passive data structure internal to the actor.
Erlang~\cite{erlang-2010}, for instance, provides mailbox interaction only
through low-level system primitives with opaque
handling~\cite{formal-semantics-erlang}. Actor-as-object
models~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models} similarly treat mailboxes as internal
components with limited exposed functionality beyond basic queue operations. The
Kilim framework~\cite{kilim-framework} takes a step towards separation by
decoupling mailboxes from actors through specialised queues, but still treats
them as passive data structures rather than first-class entities. Recent work on
mailbox types~\cite{special-delivery-mailbox-types-2023} enriches the type
system to reason about mailbox contents, but does not promote the mailbox itself
to actor status.

This design choice has consequences for formal reasoning. When the mailbox is
opaque, one cannot specify, at the level of the model, properties such as
message filtering, prioritisation, or custom delivery policies. Nor can one
reason modularly about the interaction between message storage and message
processing. Conversely, when the mailbox is a passive data structure
(as in Kilim), one gains structural separation but not behavioural
independence: the mailbox cannot react to system events, enforce delivery
invariants, or evolve its strategy without modifications to the actor runtime.

We propose promoting mailboxes to independent, first-class actors---native
actors in the system with their own configuration, behaviour, and lifecycle.
Each processing actor is automatically paired with a dedicated \emph{mailbox
actor} that manages message storage and delivery. Communication occurs through
message passing to the mailbox actor, and the processing actor retrieves
messages only when it is ready to handle them. This pull-based mechanism
implements natural backpressure and enables flexible, replaceable mailbox
implementations. Crucially, the mailbox actor is not a passive data
structure: it is a fully-fledged actor with its own guarded-action behaviour,
enabling custom delivery policies, message prioritisation, and filtering
without modifying the processing actor or the system runtime.

We formalise this architecture in a predicative Martin-L\"of type theory with
dependent types. The choice of foundation is driven by the model itself: every
engine component---messages, configurations, environments, behaviours---is a
type family indexed by a finite type of engine type indices, so a type-safe
description of engine creation and inter-engine communication requires dependent
types to quantify over indices and pair them with their associated data.
Alternative foundations such as set theory or rewriting logic could express the
operational rules but would not enforce these typing invariants by construction;
dependent types make them intrinsic, yielding definitions that are correct by
construction and directly implementable in proof assistants such as Agda, Coq,
or Lean. Our model introduces \emph{engines}---a refinement of the actor
notion---structured around four components: a configuration, an execution
environment, a message interface, and a behaviour defined by guarded actions.

\paragraph{Contributions}
The contributions of this paper are threefold.
First, we present a dependent-type-theoretic formalisation of actor systems in
which mailboxes are first-class actors (\Cref{sec:model}). The framework is
parametric in the engine type family, making it applicable to any actor-like
system following a process-oriented paradigm.
Second, we provide a complete operational semantics expressed as a labelled
transition system (\Cref{sec:semantics}), covering engine creation with
automatic mailbox pairing, message passing, behaviour evaluation under
non-overlapping guards, effect execution, and engine lifecycle management.
Third, we establish several metatheoretic properties of the system
(\Cref{sec:properties}), including type preservation under reduction, progress,
effect determinism, and mailbox isolation.

\paragraph{Outline}
\Cref{sec:type-theory} fixes notation and conventions.
\Cref{sec:model} introduces the engine model and its components, culminating in
the definition of mailbox engines. \Cref{sec:semantics} presents the
operational semantics. \Cref{sec:properties} states metatheoretic properties.
\Cref{sec:related-work} discusses related work, and \Cref{sec:conclusion}
concludes.

\section{Notation}\label{sec:type-theory}

We work in a predicative Martin-L\"of type theory with a countable hierarchy of
universes
\begin{equation*}
  \Ui{0} : \Ui{1} : \Ui{2} : \cdots
\end{equation*}
where $\Ui{n} : \Ui{n+1}$ for all $n \in \N$. The symbol $\U$ denotes
$\Ui{0}$ when the universe level is not important. All constructions in this
paper live in $\Ui{0}$ unless stated otherwise.

\paragraph{$\Pi$-types}
Given a type $\typing{A}{\Ui{n}}$ and a type family
$\typing{B}{A \to \Ui{m}}$, the $\Pi$-type (dependent function type)
$\Pi_{(x : A)}\, B(x)$ inhabits $\Ui{\max(n,m)}$. When $B$ does not depend on
$x$, we write $A \to B$.

\paragraph{$\Sigma$-types}
Given $\typing{A}{\Ui{n}}$ and $\typing{B}{A \to \Ui{m}}$, the $\Sigma$-type
(dependent pair type) $\Sigma_{(x : A)}\, B(x)$ inhabits $\Ui{\max(n,m)}$. Its
terms are denoted $\langle a, b \rangle$ where $\typing{a}{A}$ and
$\typing{b}{B(a)}$. When $B$ does not depend on $x$, we write $A \times B$ and
call its terms \emph{tuples}. We generalise so that
$\langle a_1, \dots, a_n \rangle$ is a tuple of type
$A_1 \times \cdots \times A_n$.

\paragraph{Inductive types}
We use inductive type definitions in the following form:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:inductive-type-definition}
  \typedef{
    \ctype{A}
  }{
    \cdots \mid \ccon{B} \mid \cdots \mid \ccon{C}(A_1, \dots, A_m) \mid \cdots
  }\,,
\end{equation}
where $\ccon{B}$ and $\ccon{C}$ are constructors of $\ctype{A}$, with
$\ccon{B}$ nullary and $\ccon{C}$ taking $m$ arguments. The type $\ctype{A}$
may also be indexed by parameters. Pattern matching is used for elimination.

\paragraph{Standard types}
We denote by $\N$ the type of natural numbers, $\EmptyType$ the empty type,
$\Unit$ the unit type with its unique term $*$, $\Bool$ the two-element type
with truth values $\mathsf{tt}$ and $\mathsf{ff}$, $\List{A}$ the type of lists
over $A$, $\Fin{n}$ the type with exactly $n$ elements, and $\Option{A}$ the
optional type $A \oplus \Unit$ with constructors $\none$ and $\some{a}$ for
$\typing{a}{A}$. The empty list is written $[]$ and $[a_1, \dots, a_n]$ denotes
a list of $n$ elements.

\paragraph{Equalities and notation}
We write $\defequal{x}{y}$ for definitional equality (defining $x$ to be
$y$), $\equal{a}{b}$ for judgmental equality (holding by computation), and
$\propequal{a}{b}$ for propositional equality (the identity type). In inference
rules, premises use judgmental equality unless otherwise noted. A typing
judgement is denoted $\typing{x}{A}$. We write $(\ignore)$ to omit terms that
are irrelevant or inferable from context.

\paragraph{Finite subsets and set-theoretic notation}
Certain types in our model are equipped with decidable equality. For such types,
we freely use set-theoretic notation as syntactic sugar. Finite subsets of a
type $A$ are formalised as $\FinSet{A}$, implemented as lists with a uniqueness
invariant. We write $a \in S$ for membership, $S \cup T$ for union, and
$S \setminus T$ for difference. The disjoint union of types $A_1, \dots, A_n$
is denoted $A_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_n$.

\paragraph{Functions}
A function $\typing{f}{A \to B}$ applied to $a$ is written $f(a)$.
The \emph{function update} $f[a \mapsto c]$ denotes the function agreeing with
$f$ except that input $a$ maps to $c$. We overload this notation to finite
sets: $S[a \mapsto c]$ replaces element $a$ by $c$ in $S$.

\section{Model}\label{sec:model}

This section presents a formal model of actor systems in which mailboxes are
promoted to first-class actors. Building on the actor-like semantics
of~\cite{art-engines}, we formalise \emph{engines} as refined communicating
Moore machines structured around four core components: a \emph{configuration},
an \emph{execution environment}, a \emph{message interface}, and a
\emph{behaviour}. These components, more precisely their types, define the type
signature for engines in a system, as detailed in~\Cref{def:engine}.

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{figures/engine}
  \caption{
  An engine $e$ processing a message $m$ conforming to its message interface
  $\msgtype{e}$, producing an effect by evaluating its behaviour $\behaviour{e}$
  in the context of its configuration $\config{e}$ and execution environment
  $\environment{e}$. The dashed line indicates that these components may change
  in a future step. The behaviour $\behaviour{e}$ consists of guarded actions
  $\langle g_1, a_1 \rangle, \langle g_2, a_2 \rangle$, and
  $\langle g_3, a_3 \rangle$.}
  \label{fig:engine}
\end{figure}

The configuration conveys persistent metadata about the engine such as its
parent reference and operational mode (\Cref{def:engine-configuration}). The
execution environment contains the engine's local state and an address book of
other engines (\Cref{def:engine-environment}). The behaviour specifies how the
engine reacts to messages via \emph{guarded actions}---pairs of
precondition/action that, when triggered, produce \emph{effects}: commands
requesting the system to send messages, update state, spawn new engines, or
terminate (\Cref{def:effects}).

For guarded action evaluation, various strategies exist for selecting which
action to execute when multiple guards are satisfied. We present one such
strategy based on non-overlapping guards in \Cref{sec:behavior-eval-rules}.

\begin{remark}[Event-Driven Systems]\label{rem:event-driven-systems}%
  The behaviour of engines resembles the correct-by-construction approach used
  in event-driven systems such as Event-B~\cite{dupont:tel-04165215}. In that
  approach, system transitions are driven by events consisting of preconditions
  on the current state and actions to execute when these preconditions hold.
  Our guarded actions play an analogous role, with the effect system
  (\Cref{def:effects}) restricting the available actions.
\end{remark}

All type families in the model are indexed by a finite type of engine type
indices, which we now introduce.

\begin{definition}[Engine Type Index]\label{def:engine-type-index}%
We postulate a finite type $\typing{\EngIdx}{\U}$ of \textbf{engine type
indices}. For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, the system defines the associated
type families $\msgtype{i}$, $\configtype{i}$, $\envtype{i}$, and
$\behaviourtype{i}$.
\end{definition}

The finiteness of $\EngIdx$ reflects the assumption that engine types are fixed
when the system is defined. Adding support for dynamic type extension is left
for future work.

\subsection{Preliminaries}\label{sec:preliminaries}%

The model operates on a system state $\SystemState$ consisting of finite sets
of uniquely identified nodes $\SetOfNodes$, engines $\SetOfEngines$, and
in-transit messages $\SetOfMessages$. The subsequent sections detail these
concepts in a bottom-up manner.

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/system}
  \caption{A system state $\SystemState$ with nodes $\SetOfNodes$ (dashed
  regions), engines $\SetOfEngines$ (boxes), and in-transit messages
  $\SetOfMessages$ (arrows). The message
  $\msgpacket{\loc{1}{e_{s}}}{\loc{2}{e_{t}}}{m}$ is in transit from engine
  $e_{s}$ on node $1$ to engine $e_{t}$ on node $2$.}
  \label{fig:system-state}
\end{figure}

As a state machine, the system evolves in discrete steps, where the state at
step $i$ is denoted $\SystemState^{(i)}$. The sets $\SetOfNodes^{(i)}$,
$\SetOfEngines^{(i)}$, and $\SetOfMessages^{(i)}$ may change between steps.
When no confusion arises, we omit the step index. Each engine resides on a
unique node, as formalised in \Cref{def:engine,def:node}.

\begin{remark}[Static Engine Allocation]\label{rem:move-engines}%
  The model assumes that engines remain on the node where they were created.
  Engine migration between nodes is not considered.
\end{remark}

Communication between engines occurs via typed messages carried by the system.
A message-passing mechanism governs the delivery of in-transit messages
$\SetOfMessages$, as described in \Cref{sec:message-structure}. Each message
contains sender and recipient identifiers along with a typed payload.

\subsection{Addresses}\label{sec:addresses-and-names}

\begin{definition}[Address]%
\label{def:address}
The \textbf{address} of an engine is a pair $\loc{n}{\iota}$ of type
$\Address$ where $n$ is the node identifier and $\iota$ is the engine's unique
identifier within that node. Formally,
\begin{equation}
  \defequal{\Address}{\N \times \N}\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Message Structure}\label{sec:message-structure}

Engines communicate via typed messages. The type system ensures that every
message payload conforms to the target engine's interface.

\begin{definition}[Message Interface]\label{def:message-interface}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, the \textbf{message interface} $\msgtype{i}$ is
a type whose constructors, called \textbf{message tags}, wrap payload types that
engines of type $i$ can process. Each message tag carries a name describing its
purpose, analogous to method names in object-oriented systems.
\end{definition}

The message interface $\msgtype{i}$ is presented as an inductive type in the
style of \eqref{eq:inductive-type-definition}. For a concrete system, each
engine type index $i$ fixes its message interface.

Type-safe communication is ensured by how messages are defined: there is no way
to construct an ill-formed message.

\begin{definition}[Message]\label{def:messages}
A \textbf{message} is a triple $\langle s, t, v \rangle$ denoted
$\msgpacket{s}{t}{v}$ where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\typing{s}{\Address}$ is the sender engine address,
    \item $\typing{t}{\Address}$ is the target engine address, and
    \item $\typing{v}{\msgtype{t}}$ is the payload conforming to the target
    engine's message interface.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

The type of the payload $v$ depends on the target address $t$: it must conform
to $\msgtype{t}$, the message interface of the engine at address $t$. This
dependent typing ensures that only well-formed messages can be constructed.

To refer to the type of an arbitrary message within the system, we use the
$\Sigma$-type
\begin{equation}\label{eq:message-type-sum}
  \defequal{\TotalMsg}{\Sigma_{(\typing{i}{\EngIdx})}\,\msgtype{i}}\,,
\end{equation}
which collects all valid message payloads across engine types.

\subsection{Engine Components}\label{sec:engine-components}

We now define the components that constitute an engine. For each engine type
index $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, these components are given as type families
indexed by $i$.

\begin{definition}[Engine Lifecycle]\label{def:engine-status}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, the \textbf{status} of an engine of type $i$ is
a term of type $\EngineStatus{i}$, determining the engine's operational state
within its lifecycle.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-status}
\begin{aligned}
\typedef%
  {\EngineStatus{i}}%
  {    &\quad \ready{\msgtype{i} \to \Bool} \\%
  \mid & \quad \busy{\msgtype{i}} \\%
  \mid & \quad \terminated  \, .
  }
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}

\begin{figure}%[!hbp]
\centering
\[\begin{tikzcd}[row sep=1em, column sep=2em]
	& {\textcolor{darkgreen}{\ready{f}}} \\
	{\textcolor{darkred}{\terminated}} && {\textcolor{darkorange}{\busy{m}}}
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-1]
	\arrow[from=1-2, to=2-3, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=1-2, bend right]
	\arrow[from=2-3, to=2-1, bend left]
\end{tikzcd}\]%
\caption{The engine lifecycle: an engine starts in $\ready{f}$ state, where $f$
is a predicate indicating which messages the engine accepts. Upon receiving a
message $m$, it transitions to $\busy{m}$. From there, it either returns to
$\ready{g}$ with a (possibly different) predicate $g$, or transitions to
$\terminated$.}
\label{fig:engine-status}
\end{figure}
\end{definition}

When an engine is in the $\ready{f}$ state, it is available to process incoming
messages. The predicate $\typing{f}{\msgtype{i} \to \Bool}$ acts as a filter,
determining which messages the engine will accept. By default, a newly created
engine starts in the $\ready{\lambda x.\mathsf{tt}}$ state, accepting all
messages.

After creation, the engine may transition to $\busy{m}$, where it is actively
processing message $m$, or to $\terminated$, where it stops processing and is
subsequently removed from the system.

We distinguish between two operational modes of engines.

\begin{definition}[Operational Mode]\label{def:engine-mode}
The \textbf{operational mode} of an engine is of type $\EngineMode$,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-mode}
\typedef{\EngineMode}
  {    \process
  \mid \mail
  }\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

The operational mode is fixed at creation and opaque to the engine itself. The
system's message-passing mechanism treats engines differently based on their
mode: messages sent to \process{} engines are internally routed to their paired
\mail{} engines, which manage message storage and delivery.

\begin{definition}[Configuration]\label{def:engine-configuration}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, we postulate a type $\typing{C_i}{\U}$ of
engine-type-specific configuration data. The \textbf{configuration} of an
engine of type $i$ is a tuple $\configval{r}{\mode}{c}$ of type
$\configtype{i}$, where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\typing{r}{\Option{\Address}}$ is an optional reference to the
    engine's parent,
    \item $\typing{\mode}{\EngineMode}$ is the engine's operational mode, and
    \item $\typing{c}{C_i}$ is configuration data specific to the engine type.
\end{itemize}
The type family of configurations is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-configuration-type}
\defequal{
  \configtype{i}
}{
         \Option{\Address}
  \times \EngineMode
  \times C_i
}\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

For an engine $p$ of type $i$, we write $\config{p}$ for its configuration
value and $\configtype{i}$ for the type.

\begin{definition}[Execution Environment]\label{def:engine-environment}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, we postulate a type $\typing{L_i}{\U}$ for
the local state. The execution \textbf{environment} of an engine of type $i$
is a pair $\localenvval{s}{m}$ of type $\envtype{i}$, where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\typing{s}{L_i}$ is the local state, and
    \item $\typing{m}{\Name \to \Address}$ is the engine's address book, with
    a predefined name \self for the engine's own address.
\end{itemize}
The type family of environments is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-environment-type}
\defequal{
  \envtype{i}
}{
  L_i \times (\Name \to \Address)
}\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

For an engine $p$ of type $i$, we write $\localenv{p}$ for its environment
and $\envtype{i}$ for the type.

\subsection{Execution Directives}\label{sec:execution-directives}

The behaviour of an engine specifies what it can perform when reacting to a
message. How the engine reacts is determined by a set of guarded
actions---pairs of \emph{guards} (preconditions) and \emph{actions}. When a
message is processed, the system evaluates it against the engine's guards in
the context of the engine's environment and configuration. If a guard is
satisfied, its associated action produces an \emph{effect}: a command to the
system.

\begin{definition}[Effect System]\label{def:effects}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, an \textbf{effect} triggered by an engine of
type $i$ is a term of type $\effecttype{i}$. Each effect represents a primitive
operation that the issuing engine requests the system to execute.
\begin{equation}\label{eq:effects}
\begin{aligned}
\typedef{%
  &\effecttype{i}
}{\\   &\quad \noop & &%
        \text{(null operation)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \send{\Address}{\TotalMsg} & &%
        \text{(message dispatch)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \update{\envtype{i}} & &%
        \text{(update environment)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \spawn{
    \Sigma_{(j : \EngIdx)}\, (\configtype{j} \times \envtype{j})
  } & &%
        \text{(spawn child engine of type $j$)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \mfilter{%
                  {\msgtype{i} \to \Bool}
              }& &%
        \text{(set mailbox filter)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \terminate & &%
        \text{(engine shutdown)} \\
\, \mid & \quad \chain{\effecttype{i}}{\effecttype{i}} & &%
        \text{(effect sequencing)\, .}
}
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{figure}%[!htbp]
\[\begin{tikzcd}[row sep=small, column sep=5mm]
	{} & {(m, \config{p},\localenv{p})} & {} \\
	\\
	{r_1} & \cdots & {r_k} \\
	{e_1:\effecttype{i}} & \cdots & {e_k:\effecttype{i}}
	\arrow[curve={height=24pt}, shorten <=24pt, shorten >=24pt, dashed, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g_1}"', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-1]
	\arrow[""', maps to, from=1-2, to=3-2]
	\arrow["{g_k}", maps to, from=1-2, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{c_1}\,\,"', maps to, from=3-1, to=4-1]
	\arrow[maps to, from=3-2, to=4-2]
	\arrow["\,\,{c_k}", maps to, from=3-3, to=4-3]
\end{tikzcd}\]%
\caption{For an engine $p$ of type $i$, its behaviour acts on a message $m$
of type $\msgtype{i}$, configuration $\config{p}$, and environment
$\localenv{p}$. The guards $g_k$ produce optional values $r_k$ that feed into
actions $c_k$, which return effects of type $\effecttype{i}$.}
\label{fig:effect}
\end{figure}

Effects serve as the declarative interface specifying what an engine can do,
without exposing how the behaviour computes its response.

\begin{definition}[Guard]\label{def:guards}%
Given a type $\returntype$, a \textbf{guard} for an engine of type $i$ is a
function of type $\In{i} \to \Option{\returntype}$ where
\begin{equation}\label{eq:guard-type-in}
\defequal{\In{i}}{\msgtype{i} \times \configtype{i} \times \envtype{i}}\,.
\end{equation}
Applied to a message, configuration, and environment, a guard returns
$\some{r}$ if satisfied, and $\none$ otherwise.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Action]\label{def:action}%
An \textbf{action} for an engine of type $i$ acting on data of type
$\returntype$ is a function of type
\begin{equation}\label{eq:action-type-out}
\returntype \times
\In{i}
\to
\Out{i} \text{ where }
\defequal{\Out{i}}{\effecttype{i}}\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

Together, a guard and an action induce a function of type $\In{i} \to \Out{i}$.

\begin{definition}[Guarded Action]\label{def:guarded-action}%
Given a guard $\guard{i}$ and an action $\action{i}$ for an engine of type $i$,
the \textbf{guarded action} $\guard{i} \odot \action{i}$ is the function of
type $\In{i} \to \Out{i}$ defined by
\begin{equation}\label{eq:composite-guard-action-operation}
  \begin{aligned}
  \defequal{
    &(\guard{i} \odot \action{i})
  }{
  \lambda \langle m, c, s \rangle .
    \mathsf{case}\ \guard{i}(m)\ \mathsf{of}\ \{ \\
     &\quad \some{r} \Rightarrow \action{i}(r, \langle m, c, s \rangle); \\
     &\quad \none \Rightarrow \noop
  \}
  \,.
  }
  \end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Dependent Action Type]\label{rem:dependent-action-type}%
The action component is only invoked when the guard returns $\some{r}$.
In a dependently-typed setting, one can refine the action type to
$\Pi\,(x : \In{i}).\, g(x) = \some{r} \to \Out{i}$, recording
in the type system that the action is defined only when the guard holds.
This eliminates the need for a separate guard-match assumption in
semantic rules such as \bruleref{GuardedActionEval}.  Our Lean~4
formalisation employs this refinement.
\end{remark}

\begin{definition}[Engine Behaviour]\label{def:engine-behaviour}%
A \textbf{behaviour} for an engine of type $i$ is a list of guarded actions,
of type $\behaviourtype{i}$, where
\begin{equation}
\defequal{
  \behaviourtype{i}
}{
  \List{
    \In{i} \to \Out{i}
  }
}\,.
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

To ensure deterministic evaluation, we require that behaviours satisfy a
well-formedness condition.

\begin{definition}[Non-Overlapping Guards]\label{def:non-overlapping-guards}%
A behaviour $\typing{b}{\behaviourtype{i}}$ has \textbf{non-overlapping guards}
if for every input $\typing{x}{\In{i}}$, at most one guarded action in $b$
produces a non-$\noop$ effect. That is, for all
$\guardedactionval{g_j}{a_j}, \guardedactionval{g_k}{a_k} \in b$ with $j \neq k$,
if $g_j(x) = \some{r}$ then $g_k(x) = \none$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Well-Formed Behaviour]\label{def:well-formed-behaviour}%
A \textbf{well-formed behaviour} for an engine of type $i$ is a pair
$\typing{(b, \pi)}{\behaviourtype{i} \times \mathsf{NonOverlapping}(b)}$
consisting of a behaviour $b$ and a proof $\pi$ that $b$ has non-overlapping
guards. Throughout this paper, all behaviours are well-formed.
\end{definition}

Alternative guard selection strategies---inspired, for instance, by
Dijkstra's guarded command
language~\cite{dijkstra-guarded-commands}---are compatible with the model
but fall outside the scope of this work.

\begin{remark}[Evolving Behaviours]\label{rem:fixed-behaviour}%
In the present model, behaviours are fixed at engine creation.
One could extend the effect system with an additional constructor
for changing behaviours, at the cost of additional complexity in the
operational semantics.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Engines}\label{sec:processing-engines}

Engines are the core computational units of the system. Each engine processes
messages of a specific type in isolation and produces effects instructing the
system on actions to take. Unlike actor-as-objects and actors-as-processes
models~\cite{taxonomy-of-actor-models}, engines are characterised by their
typed components and the declarative nature of their behaviour.

\begin{definition}[Engine Type]\label{def:engine-type}%
For each $\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, the \textbf{engine type} $\enginetype{i}$
specifies a message interface and a well-formed behaviour
(\Cref{def:well-formed-behaviour}):
  \begin{equation}\label{eq:engine-type-definition}
    \begin{aligned}
    \defequal{
     \enginetype{i}
    }{
      \msgtype{i}
      \times \behaviourtype{i}
    }\,.
    \end{aligned}
  \end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Engine]\label{def:engine}%
An \textbf{engine} of type $\enginetype{i}$ is a tuple
$\engineval{\msgtype{i}}{\behaviourtype{i}}{o}{c}{s}$, where:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\typing{o}{\EngineStatus{i}}$: the engine's status,
  \item $\typing{c}{\configtype{i}}$: the engine's configuration, and
  \item $\typing{s}{\envtype{i}}$: the engine's execution environment.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

For clarity, as shown in \Cref{fig:engine}, we include the message interface
and behaviour in the tuple representation, highlighting the engine's type.

\subsection{Mailbox Engines}\label{sec:mailbox-engines}

As established in \Cref{def:engine-mode}, the system includes two kinds of
engines: processing engines (mode $\process$) and mailbox engines (mode
$\mail$). A mailbox engine is itself an engine---a native actor in the
system---with its own configuration, environment, and behaviour. The system
automatically creates a mailbox engine when a processing engine is spawned
(\Cref{sec:creation-rules}). Users cannot directly create mailbox engines,
though they may provide custom configuration, environment, and behaviour for
the mailbox at the moment of processing engine creation. The default mailbox
operates as a first-in-first-out queue.

\begin{figure}
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/mailbox-engines}
  \caption{A processing engine $p$ with its dedicated mailbox engine
  $\mailboxOfname$. When a message $m_k$ is sent to $p$, the system routes it
  to $\mailboxOfname$, which stores it. When $p$ is ready, the mailbox
  delivers a message determined by $p$'s filter and the mailbox's behaviour.}
  \label{fig:mailbox-engines}
\end{figure}

\begin{definition}[Mailbox Engine]\label{def:mailbox-engine}%
A \emph{mailbox engine} $m$ for a processing engine $p$ of type $i$ is a
system-configured engine satisfying:
\begin{itemize}
\item The operational mode is $\mail$.
\item The parent reference points to $p$.
\item The message interface is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:mailbox-engine-message-interface}
  \defequal{
    \msgtype{m}
  }{
    \append(\msgtype{i})
  }\,.
\end{equation}
\item The local state of type $L_m$ implements a message store with:
  \begin{itemize}
  \item $\emptyset$ representing the empty state,
  \item $\typing{(\insertmsgop)}{L_m \times \msgtype{i} \to L_m}$ for
  message insertion, and
  \item $\typing{(\deletemsgop)}{L_m \times \msgtype{i} \to L_m}$ for
  message deletion.
  \end{itemize}
\item The default behaviour $b_m$ consists of a single guarded action
  $\guardedactionval{g_m}{a_m}$ where:
  \begin{itemize}
  \item $g_m(\append(v), c, q) \coloneqq \some{v}$\,: the guard always
  matches, extracting the payload $v$ from the $\append$ wrapper.
  \item $a_m(v, \append(v), c, q) \coloneqq \update{q \cup \{v\}}$\,: the
  action stores the payload in the message store via an $\ccon{Update}$ effect.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

Since the mailbox engine is a native actor, it processes incoming messages
through its behaviour via \sruleref{Process}, just as any other engine.
The \mruleref{Enqueue} rule transitions the mailbox engine from $\ready{f}$ to
$\busy{w}$, after which the behaviour evaluates the message and produces an
effect---by default, storing the payload in the message store.

As depicted in \Cref{fig:mailbox-engines}, adding mailbox engines introduces a
clear producer-consumer pattern. The separation of message storage from
processing logic, opaque to sending engines, allows for independent scaling of
message ingestion and core computation. The mailbox engine acts as a mediator
implementing custom delivery policies via its behaviour, while the processing
engine focuses on its computational tasks.

\begin{remark}[Mailbox Overhead for Simple Engines]\label{rem:mailbox-overhead}%
  For processing engines that require no custom delivery logic, one may ask
  whether the mailbox indirection introduces unnecessary overhead. In our
  design, the default mailbox is a simple first-in-first-out queue that
  requires no configuration from the engine implementor: it is created
  automatically, accepts all messages, and is transparent to sending engines.
  Thus the ``cost'' for simple engines is purely in the formalism---an
  additional engine instance exists---not in the user's modelling effort. The
  benefit is that the system uniformly supports mailbox customisation when
  engines evolve beyond the simple case, without requiring architectural
  changes.
\end{remark}

\input{causal_mailbox.tex}

\subsection{Nodes}\label{sec:nodes}

\begin{definition}[Node]\label{def:node}%
A \textbf{node} in $\SetOfNodes$ is a tuple
$\nodeval{n_i}{\enginemap_i}{\plugins_i}$ where $\node{i}$ is the node's
unique identifier, $\enginemap_i$ maps local identifiers to engine instances,
and $\plugins_i$ represents node-specific services such as unique identifier
generation.
\end{definition}

For a node with identifier $n_i$, we write $\SetOfEngines_i$ for the set of
engines on that node (the values of $\enginemap_i$), and $\alpha_i$ for the
mapping from engine instances to their addresses.

\subsection{System}\label{sec:system}%

The system is a collection of interacting engines residing in nodes. For each
$\typing{i}{\EngIdx}$, the system defines a message interface $\msgtype{i}$,
configuration type $\configtype{i}$, environment type $\envtype{i}$, behaviour
type $\behaviourtype{i}$, and engine type $\enginetype{i}$. These types are
fixed throughout the system's lifetime and constitute the typing context.

The system structure resembles a Kripke model. We define the possible system
states and then specify transition rules governing the system's evolution.

\subsection{System State}\label{sec:state}%

\begin{definition}[System State]\label{def:state}%
A \textbf{system state} $\SystemState$ is a tuple
$\systemval{\SetOfNodes}{\SetOfMessages}{\SetOfPlugins}$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\SetOfNodes$ is the set of uniquely identified nodes,
\item $\SetOfMessages$ is the set of in-transit messages, and
\item $\SetOfPlugins$ is the set of system-wide services.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

The superscript $\SystemState^{(i)}$ denotes the system state at step $i$,
with $\SystemState^{(0)}$ the initial state. We may define
$\defequal{\SystemState^{(0)}}{\systemval{\emptyset}{\emptyset}{\emptyset}}$
and grow the system via the transition rules of \Cref{sec:semantics}.

The system services $\SetOfPlugins$ include:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\freshidname$: generates unique identifiers, and
\item $\typing{\mailboxOfname}{\Address \to \Address}$: returns the address
of the dedicated mailbox engine for a given processing engine.
\end{itemize}

We extend the node-local engine map $\enginemap_i$ to a global version
$\enginemap$ that operates over all engines $\SetOfEngines$ in the system.

\section{Operational Semantics}\label{sec:semantics}

The operational semantics are expressed through the reduction relation
$\stepsymbol$, where
$\sysstep{\SystemState^{(i)}}{\SystemState^{(i+1)}}$ indicates a transition.
A system state $\kappa$ is represented as
$(\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)$, with $\enginemap$ mapping
engine addresses to instances, and $\alpha$ mapping instances to addresses.

\subsection{Judgment Forms}\label{sec:judgment-forms}

The operational semantics employ five judgment forms, organised by domain.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{System-level operations.}
$\opstep{\SystemState, \cdots}{op}{\SystemState'}$
states that system operation $\mathsf{op}$ transforms $\SystemState$ into
$\SystemState'$.

\item \textbf{Behaviour evaluation.}
$\evalstep{p}{v}{E}$
states that engine $p$ processing message $v$ produces effect $E$.

\item \textbf{Guarded action evaluation.}
$\guardevalmsgstep{p}{g}{a}{v}{E}$
states that in the context of engine $p$, the guarded action
$\langle g, a \rangle$ produces effect $E$ when processing message $v$.

\item \textbf{Effect execution.}
$\effectevalstep{\SystemState}{E}{\SystemState'}$
states that executing effect $E$ in state $\SystemState$ produces state
$\SystemState'$.

\item \textbf{Engine processing.}
$\processstep{\SystemState}{p}{m}{\SystemState'}$
states that in state $\SystemState$, engine $p$ processing message $m$
yields state $\SystemState'$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{System-level Initialisation}\label{sec:creation-rules}

\begin{definition}[\srule{Node}]\label{rule:s-Node}
A new node is created with a fresh identifier and an empty engine map.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      \defequal{\iota}{\freshid}
\quad %
      \equal{%
        n
      }{%
        \nodeval%
          {\iota}
          {\emptyset}
          {\plugins_n}
      }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
{\SystemState}%
{node}%
{%
  ( \SetOfNodes \cup \{n\}
  , \SetOfMessages
  , \SetOfPlugins
  )
}%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engine Creation}\label{sec:engine-creation-rules}

\sloppy
Engine creation involves setting up both the processing engine and its
dedicated mailbox engine. The following auxiliary rule creates an individual
engine; it is not a top-level system transition but is invoked by
\sruleref{SpawnWithMailbox}.

\begin{definition}[\srule{EngineSpawn} (auxiliary)]\label{rule:s-EngineSpawn}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$%
        \equal{%
          \node{i}
        }{%
          \nodeval%
            {\mathsf{id}_i}
            {\enginemap_i}
            {\plugins_i}
        }
        \in \SetOfNodes
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
        \typing{k}{\EngIdx}
  \quad \typing{b}{\behaviourtype{k}}
  \quad \typing{f}{\msgtype{k} \to \Bool}%
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
        \typing{\pi}{\Option{\Address}} %
  \quad %
        \equal{%
          \typing{
            \config{\new}
            }{\configtype{k}}%
        }{%
          \configval%
            {\pi}
            {o}
            {c}
        }%
  $}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{%
          \typing{\localenv{\new}}{\envtype{k}}
      }{
        \localenvval%
          {s}
          {[(\textsc{self}, \iota)]}
      }
\quad \defequal{\iota}{\freshid}
\quad \typing{\loc{\mathsf{id}_i}{\iota}}{\Address}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \equal{%
    \engine{\new}
  }{
    \engineval%
      {\msgtype{k}} %
      {b} %
      {\ready{f}} %
      {\config{\new}} %
      {\environment{\new}}
  }
\quad
  \equal{%
          \enginemap'
        }{
          \enginemap_i%
            [\iota \mapsto \engine{\mathsf{new}} ]%
        }%
$}
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \opstep%
    { \SystemState  %
    , \node{i}
    , k
    , \pi
    , \iota
    , b
    , f
    , o
    , c
    , s
    }
    {spawn}%
    {%
      ( \SetOfNodes[\enginemap_i \mapsto \enginemap']%
      , \SetOfMessages%
      , \SetOfPlugins%
      )
    }%
  $}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

Spawning a processing engine requires creating its paired mailbox engine. The
following rule makes this coupling explicit.

\begin{definition}[\srule{SpawnWithMailbox}]\label{rule:s-SpawnWithMailbox}
Every processing engine is paired with a dedicated mailbox engine at creation.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  \typing{k}{\EngIdx}
\quad
  \node{i} \in \SetOfNodes
\quad
  \defequal{\iota_p}{\freshid}
\quad
  \defequal{\iota_m}{\freshid}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \opstep%
    {\SystemState, \node{i}, k, \pi, \iota_p, b_k, f, \process, c, s}
    {spawn}
    {\SystemState_1}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \opstep%
    {\SystemState_1, \node{i}, k_m, \some{\loc{\mathsf{id}_i}{\iota_p}},
     \iota_m, b_m, \lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}, \mail, c_m, s_m}
    {spawn}
    {\SystemState_2}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \opstep%
    {\SystemState, \node{i}, k, \pi, b_k, f, c, s}
    {spawn\text{-}mbox}
    {\SystemState_2}
$}
\end{prooftree}
Here $k_m$ is the engine type index for the mailbox engine paired with
processing engines of type $k$, $b_m$ is the default mailbox behaviour, and
$c_m, s_m$ are the initial mailbox configuration and state.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engine Garbage Collection}\label{sec:engine-termination}

To prevent zombie engine instances---engines in the $\terminated$ state---the
system removes them.

\begin{definition}[\srule{Clean}]\label{rule:s-Clean}
An engine whose status is $\terminated$ is removed from its node's engine map.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  \node{i} \in \SetOfNodes
\quad %
  p \in \SetOfEngines_i%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    \status{p}
  }{%
    \terminated
  }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}%
  {clean}%
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes[\enginemap_i \mapsto \enginemap_i \setminus \{p\}]%
    , \SetOfMessages%
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

The \sruleref{Clean} rule does not remove the engine's mailbox. The mailbox
engine remains to absorb messages that may still be in transit, preventing
message loss. It can be cleaned independently once it is no longer needed.

\begin{remark}[Messages to Terminated Engines]\label{rem:terminated-messages}%
When a message in transit is addressed to a processing engine that has been
terminated and cleaned, the message's target address resolves to the (still
existing) mailbox engine. Since the mailbox engine accepts all messages via
its $\append$ interface, the message is enqueued but never dequeued, as no
processing engine exists to consume it. This design choice silently absorbs
stale messages rather than introducing error-handling complexity.
\end{remark}

\subsection{Message-Passing Mechanism}\label{sec:message-passing-mechanism}

The message-passing mechanism supports asynchronous communication with
non-blocking sends, mailbox isolation with eventual delivery, and explicit
lifecycle management.

\begin{definition}[\mrule{Send}]\label{rule:m-Send}
A message with payload $v$ conforming to the target's interface is wrapped
in the $\append$ constructor and placed in transit, addressed to the target
engine's dedicated mailbox.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
\engine{s}, \engine{t} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{\mode(\engine{t})}{\process}%
\quad %
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{t}}%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \equal{%
    \SystemState
  }{%
    (\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)
  }%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {%
    \SystemState, \alpha(\engine{t}), v%
  }
  {send}%
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes
    , \SetOfMessages \cup%
      \{
        \msgpacket%
          {\alpha(\engine{s})}
          {\mailboxOf{\enginemap(\engine{t})}}
          {\append(v)}
      \}%
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }%
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

The \mruleref{Send} rule redirects messages to the target engine's dedicated
mailbox, wrapping the payload in an $\append$ constructor. This is the only way
to add a message to $\SetOfMessages$.

\begin{definition}[\mrule{Enqueue}]\label{rule:m-Enqueue}
An in-transit message addressed to a mailbox engine is consumed from
$\SetOfMessages$, transitioning the mailbox engine from $\ready{f}$ to
$\busy{w}$. The mailbox engine then processes the message through its
behaviour via \sruleref{Process}.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  \equal{%
    m
  }{%
    \msgpacket%
      {\ignore}
      {\mailbox{t}}
      {w}
  } \in \SetOfMessages
\quad %
  \mailbox{t} \in \SetOfEngines
\quad %
  \typing{w}{\msgtype{\mailbox{t}}}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \equal{%
    \mailbox{t}
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
      {\ready{f}}
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
  }
\quad %
  \equal{f(w)}{\mathsf{tt}}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {enqueue}
  {%
    ( \SetOfNodes[
        \enginemap(\mailbox{t})
          \mapsto
        \engineval%
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
          {\busy{w}}
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
      ]
    , \SetOfMessages \setminus \{m\}
    , \SetOfPlugins%
    )
  }
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\mrule{Dequeue}]\label{rule:m-Dequeue}
When a processing engine is ready and its mailbox contains a message passing
the engine's filter, the message is removed from the mailbox and the engine
transitions to the busy state.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{\mode(p)}{\process}%
\quad %
  \equal{
    p
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
      {\ready{f}}
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$
  \mailbox{p} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{
    \mailbox{p}
  }{%
    \mailboxOf{p}
  }
\quad %
  \equal{%
    \mathsf{env}(\enginemap(\mailbox{p}))
  }{%
   q \cup \{v\}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
\quad %
  \equal{f(v)}{\mathsf{tt}}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{%
  \SetOfNodes'
}{%
  \SetOfNodes[%
    \enginemap({p})%
      \mapsto%
        \engineval%
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
          {\busy{v}}
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
    ]%
    [\mailbox{p}
        \mapsto
          \engineval%
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
          {\ignore}
          {q}
    ]%
}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\opstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {dequeue}
  {
    ( \SetOfNodes'
    , \SetOfMessages
    , \SetOfPlugins
    )
  }
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

Note that the \mruleref{Dequeue} rule requires the filter predicate $f$ to
accept the message $v$, that is, $\equal{f(v)}{\mathsf{tt}}$. A message
$v$ in the mailbox for which $\equal{f(v)}{\mathsf{ff}}$ remains in the
mailbox until the filter is updated or the engine is terminated.

\subsection{Behaviour Evaluation}\label{sec:behavior-eval-rules}

This subsection formalises how an engine evaluates its guarded actions in
response to a message.

\begin{definition}[\brule{GuardedActionEval}]\label{rule:b-GuardedActionEval}
A single guarded action is evaluated: if the guard is satisfied, the action
is applied to produce an effect.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\config{p}}
      {\localenv{p}}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \guardedactionval%
    {g}
    {a}
  \in \behaviour{p}
\quad %
  \equal{%
    g(v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})
  }{%
    \some{r}
  }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$
\guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {g}
  {a}
  {v}
  {a(r, v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\brule{NoMatchingGuard}]\label{rule:b-NoMatchingGuard}
If a guard returns $\none$, the guarded action produces $\noop$.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\config{p}}
      {\localenv{p}}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\guardedactionval{g}{a} \in \behaviour{p}%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    g(v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})
   }{%
    \none%
   }
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {g}
  {a}
  {v}
  {\noop}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\paragraph{Guard Selection Strategy}
Under the non-overlapping guard assumption (\Cref{def:non-overlapping-guards}),
at most one guarded action produces a non-$\noop$ effect for any given input.
The following rule selects the unique matching guard.

\begin{definition}[\brule{GuardStrategy}]\label{rule:b-GuardStrategy}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \guardedaction{i} \in \behaviour{p}%
\quad %
  \guardevalmsgstep%
    {p}
    {\guard{i}}
    {\action{i}}
    {v}
    {E}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\forall_{j\neq i}\,
  \guardedaction{j} \in \behaviour{p} %
  \,.\
  \guardevalmsgstep%
  {p}
  {\guard{j}}
  {\action{j}}
  {v}
  {\noop}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\evalstep%
  {p}
  {v}
  {E}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

When no guard matches the message, the overall effect is $\noop$.

\begin{definition}[\brule{AllGuardsFail}]\label{rule:b-AllGuardsFail}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{%
    p
  }{%
    \engineval%
      {\ignore}
      {\behaviour{p}}
      {\busy{v}}
      {\ignore}
      {\ignore}
  }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \forall\, \guardedaction{j} \in \behaviour{p}\,.\
  \guardevalmsgstep%
    {p}
    {\guard{j}}
    {\action{j}}
    {v}
    {\noop}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\evalstep%
  {p}
  {v}
  {\noop}
$}
\end{prooftree}
When $\behaviour{p}$ is the empty list, the universal quantifier in
\bruleref{AllGuardsFail} is vacuously satisfied, so the rule produces
$\evalstep{p}{v}{\noop}$.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Effect Execution}\label{sec:effect-execution}

Given a system state $\SystemState$, an effect $E$, and a successor state
$\SystemState'$, the judgment
$\effectevalstep{\SystemState}{E}{\SystemState'}$
states that executing $E$ in $\SystemState$ produces $\SystemState'$. This
judgment is defined by case analysis on $E$.

\begin{definition}[\erule{Send}]\label{rule:e-Send}
Executing a $\ccon{Send}$ effect dispatches a message via the \mruleref{Send}
rule.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
  \engine{s}, \engine{t} \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad %
  \equal{l_{t}}{\alpha(\engine{t})}
\quad %
  \typing{v}{\msgtype{s}}
\quad \typing{m}{\msgtype{t}}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \vdots
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
     \evalstep%
        {\engine{s}}
        {v}
        {\send{l_{t}}{m}}
\quad %
      \sendstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {l_{t}}
        {m}
        {\SystemState'}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$
\effectevalstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {\send{l_{t}}{m}}
  {\SystemState'}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Update}]\label{rule:e-Update}
Executing an $\ccon{Update}$ effect replaces the engine's execution environment
with the provided value.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
\quad \typing{s}{\envtype{p}}%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
     \evalstep%
        {p}
        {v}
        {\update{s}}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep%
  {(\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)}
  {\update{s}}
  {(\SetOfNodes[
    \enginemap(\engine{p}) \mapsto %
      \engineval%
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
        {s}
    ]%
   , \SetOfMessages
   , \SetOfPlugins
   )}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}


\begin{definition}[\erule{MFilter}]\label{rule:e-MFilter}
  \hspace{-1.5em}
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$%
  p \in \SetOfEngines%
  \quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
  \quad \typing{f}{\msgtype{p} \to \Bool}%
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \evalstep%
            {p}
            {v}
            {\mfilter{f}}%
  $}
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
  {(\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)}
  {\mfilter{f}}
  {(\SetOfNodes[
    \enginemap(p)
      \mapsto %
      \engineval%
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
        {\ready{f}}
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
    ]
    , \SetOfMessages
    , \SetOfPlugins
  )}
$}
  \end{prooftree}
Note that \eruleref{MFilter} transitions the engine from $\busy{v}$ to
$\ready{f}$, setting the filter for the next message cycle. When composed via
\eruleref{Chain}, this transition takes priority over the default return to
$\ready{\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}}$ in \sruleref{Process}.
  \end{definition}


\begin{definition}[\erule{Spawn}]\label{rule:e-Spawn}
Executing a $\ccon{Spawn}$ effect creates a child engine via
\sruleref{SpawnWithMailbox}, ensuring it is paired with a mailbox engine.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
\node{i} \in \SetOfNodes%
\quad p \in \SetOfEngines_i%
\quad
  \typing{j}{\EngIdx}
\quad \defequal{\iota}{\freshid}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \equal{c}{\configval{\alpha(p)}{\process}{\ignore}}
\quad
  \typing{s}{\envtype{j}}
\quad
  \typing{b}{\behaviourtype{j}}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \opstep%
    {\SystemState, \node{i}, j, \some{\alpha(p)}, \iota, b,
     \lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}, \process, c, s}
    {spawn\text{-}mbox}
    {\SystemState'}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
    {\SystemState}
    {\spawn{\langle j, c, s \rangle}}
    {\SystemState'}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Chain}]\label{rule:e-Chain}
Executing a $\ccon{Chain}$ effect sequences two sub-effects: the first is
executed in the current state, and the second in the resulting state.
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
     \evalstep%
        {p}
        {v}
        {\chain{\mathit{eff}_1}{\mathit{eff}_2}}%
\quad \effectevalstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {\mathit{eff}_1}
        {\SystemState'}
\quad \effectevalstep%
        {\SystemState'}
        {\mathit{eff}_2}
        {\SystemState''}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep%
  {\SystemState }
  {\chain{\mathit{eff}_1}{\mathit{eff}_2}}
  {\SystemState''}
$}
\end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Terminate}]\label{rule:e-Terminate}
\hspace{-1.5em}
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      p \in \SetOfEngines%
\quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\evalstep%
          {p}
          {v}
          {\terminate}%
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\effectevalstep
  {\SystemState}
  {\terminate}
  {(\SetOfNodes[
    \enginemap(p)
      \mapsto %
      \engineval%
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
        {\terminated}
        {\ignore}
        {\ignore}
    ]
    , \SetOfMessages
    , \SetOfPlugins
  )}
$}
\end{prooftree}
The \eruleref{Terminate} effect sets the engine's status to $\terminated$.
The \sruleref{Clean} rule subsequently removes the engine from the system.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\erule{Noop}]\label{rule:e-Noop}
Executing a $\noop$ effect leaves the system state unchanged.
  \begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{$%
        p \in \SetOfEngines%
  \quad \typing{v}{\msgtype{p}}%
  $}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
  \noLine
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \evalstep%
          {p}
          {v}
          {\noop}
  $}
  \UnaryInfC{$%
  \effectevalstep%
    {\SystemState}
    {\noop}
    {\SystemState}
  $}
  \end{prooftree}
\end{definition}

\subsection{Engine Processing}\label{sec:engine-processing}

The \sruleref{Process} rule coordinates behaviour evaluation, effect execution,
and the engine's return to the ready state.

\begin{definition}[\srule{Process}]\label{rule:s-Process}
{\small
\begin{prooftree}
\AxiomC{$%
      \node{i} \in \SetOfNodes%
\quad p \in \SetOfEngines_i%
\quad %
      \equal{
        p
      }{
        \engineval%
          {\interface{p}}
          {\behaviour{p}}
          {\busy{m}}
          {\config{p}}
          {\localenv{p}}
      }
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$\vdots$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
      \evalstep%
        {p}
        {m}
        {E}
\qquad \effectevalstep%
          {\SystemState}
          {E}
          {\SystemState' = (\SetOfNodes', \SetOfMessages', \SetOfPlugins')}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{
  p'
}{
  \enginemap'(\alpha(p))
}
\qquad
\equal{
  o'
}{
  \begin{cases}
    \ready{\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}} & \text{if } \status{p'} = \busy{\ignore} \\
    \status{p'} & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
}
$}
\noLine
\UnaryInfC{$%
\equal{
  \enginemap''
}{
  \enginemap'[ %
    \alpha(p) %
      \mapsto %
        \engineval%
          {\interface{p}}
          {\behaviour{p}}
          {o'}
          {\config{p'}}
          {\localenv{p'}}
    ]
}
$}
\UnaryInfC{$%
\processstep%
  {\SystemState}
  {p}
  {m}
  {( \SetOfNodes'[%
        \enginemap' \mapsto \enginemap''
        ]%
    , \SetOfMessages'
    , \SetOfPlugins'
  )}
$}
\end{prooftree}
}%
\end{definition}

Let $p'$ denote the engine at address $\alpha(p)$ after effect execution. If
the effects did not change the engine's status (i.e.\ it remains $\busy{\ignore}$),
\sruleref{Process} transitions it to $\ready{\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}}$. If an
\eruleref{MFilter} or \eruleref{Terminate} effect already changed the status,
that status is preserved. This design ensures that filters set by
$\ccon{MFilter}$ take effect immediately, while providing a sensible default
when no filter is specified.

\subsection{Worked Example: Causal Delivery}\label{sec:causal-delivery-trace}

We trace the operational semantics on the causal delivery mailbox of
\Cref{ex:causal-mailbox}. The scenario involves a broker engine $t$ of type
$\mathsf{broker}$ whose mailbox $\mailboxOf{\enginemap(t)}$ uses the causal
behaviour $\guardedactionval{\guard{c}}{\action{c}}$. Three topic messages
arrive: $m_1$ has no dependencies, $m_2$ depends on $m_3$, and $m_3$ has no
dependencies. They arrive in order $m_1, m_2, m_3$.

The initial mailbox state is
$q_0 = (\mathsf{dlv}{=}\emptyset,\;
         \mathsf{pnd}{=}\emptyset,\;
         \mathsf{rdy}{=}[\,])$.

\paragraph{Step 1: $m_1$ enters the system.}
A publisher $s$ sends $m_1$ with $\mathsf{deps}(m_1) = \emptyset$. By
\mruleref{Send}, since $\equal{\mode(t)}{\process}$ and
$\typing{m_1}{\msgtype{\mathsf{broker}}}$, a packet
$\msgpacket{\addr{s}}{\mailboxOf{\enginemap(t)}}{\append(m_1)}$ is added to
$\SetOfMessages$.

\paragraph{Step 2: $m_1$ is enqueued.}
By \mruleref{Enqueue}, the mailbox $\mailboxOf{\enginemap(t)}$ transitions
from $\ready{f}$ to $\busy{\append(m_1)}$.

\paragraph{Step 3: The mailbox processes $m_1$.}
\sruleref{Process} fires. \bruleref{GuardStrategy} selects the unique guarded
action: $\guard{c}(\append(m_1), c, q_0) = \some{m_1}$.
Since $\mathsf{deps}(m_1) = \emptyset \subseteq q_0.\mathsf{dlv}$, the action
produces:
\[
  \chain{%
    \update{(\mathsf{dlv} \cup \{h_1\},\; \mathsf{pnd},\;
             \mathsf{rdy} \mathbin{+\!\!+} [m_1])}%
  }{\noop}
\]
\eruleref{Chain} executes: \eruleref{Update} stores $m_1$ in the ready queue
and records $h_1$ in the delivered set; \eruleref{Noop} is the identity.
The mailbox returns to $\ready{\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}}$.
\[
  q_1 = (\mathsf{dlv}{=}\{h_1\},\;
         \mathsf{pnd}{=}\emptyset,\;
         \mathsf{rdy}{=}[m_1])
\]

\paragraph{Step 4: $m_1$ is delivered.}
By \mruleref{Dequeue}, broker $t$ transitions
$\ready{f} \to \busy{m_1}$, retrieving $m_1$ from the mailbox's ready queue.

\paragraph{Step 5: $m_2$ arrives and is buffered.}
$m_2$ enters via \mruleref{Send} and \mruleref{Enqueue}, then
\sruleref{Process} fires. The guard matches: $\guard{c}(\append(m_2), c, q_1)
= \some{m_2}$. However, $\mathsf{deps}(m_2) = \{h_3\} \not\subseteq
\{h_1\} = q_1.\mathsf{dlv}$. The action produces:
\[
  \update{(\mathsf{dlv}{=}\{h_1\},\;
           \mathsf{pnd} \cup \{h_2 \mapsto m_2\},\;
           \mathsf{rdy}{=}[\,])}
\]
\eruleref{Update} stores $m_2$ in the pending buffer.
\[
  q_2 = (\mathsf{dlv}{=}\{h_1\},\;
         \mathsf{pnd}{=}\{h_2 \mapsto m_2\},\;
         \mathsf{rdy}{=}[\,])
\]

\paragraph{Step 6: $m_3$ arrives and triggers a cascade.}
$m_3$ enters via \mruleref{Send} and \mruleref{Enqueue}, then
\sruleref{Process} fires. The guard matches. Since $\mathsf{deps}(m_3) =
\emptyset \subseteq q_2.\mathsf{dlv}$, the action first stores $m_3$ in the
ready queue. The cascade check inspects
$q_2.\mathsf{pnd}$: message $m_2$ has $\mathsf{deps}(m_2) = \{h_3\}
\subseteq \{h_1, h_3\}$, so $m_2$ is now satisfiable. The action produces:
\begin{align*}
  \refeffect{Chain}\bigl(\;
    &\update{(\mathsf{dlv} \cup \{h_3\},\;
             \mathsf{pnd},\;
             \mathsf{rdy} \mathbin{+\!\!+} [m_3])},\\
    &\update{(\mathsf{dlv} \cup \{h_3\},\;
             \mathsf{pnd} \setminus \{h_2\},\;
             \mathsf{rdy} \mathbin{+\!\!+} [m_3, m_2])}\;\bigr)
\end{align*}
\eruleref{Chain} sequences two \eruleref{Update} steps: the first adds $m_3$
to the ready queue, the second moves $m_2$ from pending to ready.
\[
  q_3 = (\mathsf{dlv}{=}\{h_1, h_3\},\;
         \mathsf{pnd}{=}\emptyset,\;
         \mathsf{rdy}{=}[m_3, m_2])
\]

\paragraph{Step 7: Causal delivery.}
Two applications of \mruleref{Dequeue} deliver $m_3$ then $m_2$ to broker
$t$---restoring causal order despite the out-of-order arrival of $m_2$ before
$m_3$.

\medskip\noindent
This trace exercises \mruleref{Send}, \mruleref{Enqueue}, \mruleref{Dequeue},
\sruleref{Process}, \bruleref{GuardStrategy}, \eruleref{Update},
\eruleref{Chain}, and \eruleref{Noop}---eight of the system's eighteen rules.
The cascade in Step~6 illustrates why first-class mailbox actors are
essential: a passive data structure could not conditionally release buffered
messages upon the arrival of their dependencies.

\subsection{System State Transition Rules}\label{sec:system-state-transition-rules}

The inference rules above collectively govern system state transitions. Every
system-level operation induces a state transition:
\begin{equation}
\opstep%
  {\SystemState, \cdots}
  {op}
  {\SystemState'}%
\quad \text{implies}%
\quad \sysstep%
        {\SystemState}
        {\SystemState'}
\end{equation}
for any operation $\mathsf{op}$. \Cref{fig:state-transition-rules}
summarises all rules.

\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  {\small
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lp{0.68\linewidth}@{}}
    \textbf{Judgment form} & \textbf{Rules} \\ \hline
    System-level operations &
        \sruleref{Node},
        \sruleref{SpawnWithMailbox},
        \sruleref{Clean},
        \mruleref{Send},
        \mruleref{Enqueue},
        \mruleref{Dequeue} \\[3pt]
    Behaviour evaluation &
        \bruleref{GuardStrategy},
        \bruleref{AllGuardsFail} \\[3pt]
    Guarded action evaluation &
        \bruleref{GuardedActionEval},
        \bruleref{NoMatchingGuard} \\[3pt]
    Effect execution &
        \eruleref{Send},
        \eruleref{Update},
        \eruleref{MFilter},
        \eruleref{Spawn},
        \eruleref{Chain},
        \eruleref{Terminate},
        \eruleref{Noop} \\[3pt]
    Engine processing &
        \sruleref{Process}
  \end{tabular}
  }%
  \caption{Rules for the operational semantics, organised by judgment form.}
  \label{fig:state-transition-rules}
\end{figure}


\section{Properties}\label{sec:properties}

We state several metatheoretic properties of the system. Full proofs are
deferred to an extended version; here we provide proof sketches.

We first define well-typedness of system states. A system state
$\SystemState = (\SetOfNodes, \SetOfMessages, \SetOfPlugins)$ is
\emph{well-typed} if:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item every engine $p \in \SetOfEngines$ of type $\enginetype{i}$ has
  $\typing{\status{p}}{\EngineStatus{i}}$,
  $\typing{\config{p}}{\configtype{i}}$, and
  $\typing{\localenv{p}}{\envtype{i}}$;
  \item every message $\msgpacket{s}{t}{v} \in \SetOfMessages$ satisfies
  $\typing{v}{\msgtype{t}}$; and
  \item for every processing engine $p$, the function $\mailboxOfname$ returns
  a valid mailbox engine address.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{proposition}[Type Preservation]\label{prop:type-preservation}
If $\sysstep{\SystemState}{\SystemState'}$ and $\SystemState$ is well-typed,
then $\SystemState'$ is well-typed.
\end{proposition}
\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]\sloppy%
By case analysis on the transition rule applied.
\sruleref{Node} adds a node with an empty engine map, trivially preserving
invariants.
\sruleref{EngineSpawn} creates an engine with correctly typed components;
\sruleref{SpawnWithMailbox} composes two such creations.
For message passing: \mruleref{Send} constructs messages with payloads
satisfying $\typing{v}{\msgtype{t}}$ by premise, wrapping in $\append$;
\mruleref{Enqueue} preserves the message type when transitioning the mailbox
engine to $\busy{w}$, where $w$ has type $\msgtype{m}$;
\mruleref{Dequeue} preserves the type family when moving a message to the
processing engine's busy state.
For effect execution: \eruleref{Update} requires $\typing{s}{\envtype{p}}$;
\eruleref{Spawn} delegates to \sruleref{SpawnWithMailbox}; \eruleref{Terminate}
and \eruleref{MFilter} change only the status, remaining in
$\EngineStatus{i}$; \eruleref{Chain} composes two type-preserving steps;
\eruleref{Noop} is the identity. \sruleref{Process} evaluates the behaviour,
executes the resulting effect, and restores the engine to $\ready{f}$ or
preserves the status set by the effect. \sruleref{Clean} removes an engine
without affecting others.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Progress]\label{prop:progress}
A well-typed system state $\SystemState$ in which either
(i)~$\SetOfMessages \neq \emptyset$, or
(ii)~there exists an engine in state $\busy{m}$,
or (iii)~there exists an engine in state $\terminated$,
can take a step.
\end{proposition}
\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
We consider each case.
(i)~If $\SetOfMessages \neq \emptyset$, let
$\msgpacket{s}{t}{w} \in \SetOfMessages$. By well-typedness, $t$ is a valid
mailbox engine address. Since $\mailboxOf{}$ maps processing engines to their
mailboxes and the message is addressed to a mailbox, the target mailbox engine
exists. If the mailbox engine is in state $\ready{f}$, then since $f$ is
$\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}$ by default, the premises of \mruleref{Enqueue} are
satisfied. If the mailbox engine is $\busy{\ignore}$, then case~(ii) applies
to it and \sruleref{Process} fires, returning it to $\ready{\ignore}$.
(ii)~If an engine $p$ is in state $\busy{m}$, the behaviour $\behaviour{p}$
is a finite list of guarded actions. Each guard $g_j$ applied to the input
either returns $\some{r}$ or $\none$. If some guard matches,
\bruleref{GuardStrategy} applies; otherwise \bruleref{AllGuardsFail} applies.
In either case, a judgment $\evalstep{p}{m}{E}$ is derivable, and
\sruleref{Process} fires.
(iii)~If an engine $p$ has status $\terminated$, then
$\equal{\status{p}}{\terminated}$ and \sruleref{Clean} applies.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Effect Determinism]\label{prop:effect-determinism}
For any engine $p$ with a well-formed behaviour
(\Cref{def:well-formed-behaviour}) in state $\busy{v}$,
the judgment $\evalstep{p}{v}{E}$ determines $E$ uniquely.
\end{proposition}
\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
Guards are pure functions of type $\In{i} \to \Option{\returntype}$, so each
$g_j(v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})$ is determined. By the non-overlapping guard
condition bundled in the well-formed behaviour, for any input at most one guard
$g_i$ returns $\some{r}$ while all others return $\none$. Thus: if exactly one guard matches,
\bruleref{GuardStrategy} selects it uniquely and the effect is
$a_i(r, v, \config{p}, \localenv{p})$, which is determined since actions are
functions. If no guard matches, \bruleref{AllGuardsFail} yields $\noop$. These
two cases are exhaustive and mutually exclusive, so $E$ is uniquely determined.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Mailbox Isolation]\label{prop:mailbox-isolation}
A processing engine never directly receives a message from $\SetOfMessages$.
All messages are mediated by the corresponding mailbox engine.
\end{proposition}
\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
By inspection of the \mruleref{Send} rule: its premise
$\equal{\mode(\engine{t})}{\process}$ restricts the target to a processing
engine, and its conclusion redirects the message to
$\mailboxOf{\enginemap(\engine{t})}$. The \mruleref{Enqueue} rule delivers
messages only to mailbox engines by transitioning them to $\busy{w}$. The
\mruleref{Dequeue} rule is the sole mechanism by which a processing engine
transitions from $\ready{f}$ to $\busy{v}$, and it reads from the paired
mailbox engine. Hence, no rule places a message directly in a processing
engine's state.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Eventual Delivery]\label{prop:eventual-delivery}
Under a weak fairness assumption---every continuously applicable rule is
eventually applied---if a message $m \in \SetOfMessages$ targets a
non-terminated processing engine, then $m$ is eventually enqueued in the
target's mailbox.
\end{proposition}
\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
By \mruleref{Send}, the message $m$ is addressed to the target's mailbox
engine. The mailbox engine's status is independent of its parent processing
engine's termination: \sruleref{Clean} removes terminated engines, but the
mailbox engine persists until it is independently terminated
(\Cref{rem:terminated-messages}). Since the processing engine is
non-terminated, neither is its mailbox. The mailbox engine accepts all messages
(its filter is $\lambda\_.\mathsf{tt}$), so \mruleref{Enqueue} is applicable
whenever the mailbox is in state $\ready{\ignore}$. If the mailbox is
$\busy{\ignore}$, weak fairness ensures \sruleref{Process} eventually fires,
returning it to $\ready{\ignore}$. Thus \mruleref{Enqueue} is eventually
applicable and, under weak fairness, eventually applied---consuming $m$ from
$\SetOfMessages$ and transitioning the mailbox to $\busy{m}$.
\sruleref{Process} then evaluates the mailbox behaviour, storing the payload
in the mailbox's local state.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Causal Delivery and Eventual Delivery]\label{rem:causal-delivery}%
  The causal delivery mailbox of \Cref{ex:causal-mailbox} satisfies
  \Cref{prop:eventual-delivery}: since its guard always matches, every
  in-transit message is eventually enqueued and processed by the mailbox
  behaviour. However, a message buffered in the pending set is not delivered
  to the processing engine until its dependencies arrive. Guaranteeing
  \emph{causal delivery to the processing engine} requires the additional
  assumption that all dependency chains are finite and that every depended-upon
  message eventually enters the system. Under this assumption and weak
  fairness, each pending message's dependencies are eventually satisfied,
  triggering the cascade that moves it to the ready queue.
\end{remark}

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related-work}

\paragraph{Actor Model Foundations}
The actor model, introduced by Hewitt, Bishop, and
Steiger~\cite{Hewitt1973} and developed by Agha~\cite{Agha1986,foundation97agha},
provides the conceptual basis for our work. Our engine model refines the actor
concept by introducing typed components---configurations, environments, message
interfaces, and guarded-action behaviours---while preserving the fundamental
principles of encapsulation and asynchronous message passing.

\paragraph{Process Calculi}
Process calculi such as CCS and the $\pi$-calculus~\cite{milner-concurrency}
provide foundational frameworks for reasoning about concurrent communication.
Our system shares the emphasis on labelled transitions and compositional
semantics, but differs in treating message storage as a first-class concern
through mailbox engines rather than relying on channel-based communication.

\paragraph{Erlang}
The process-oriented
architecture of Erlang~\cite{erlang-2010,erlang} is the closest practical
counterpart to our model. Its formal
semantics~\cite{formal-semantics-erlang} defines processes and message
passing but treats the mailbox as an opaque runtime primitive. Our work
makes the mailbox explicit and programmable by promoting it to actor status.

\paragraph{Session and Behavioural Types}
Session types~\cite{honda2008mpst} and behavioural types for
actors~\cite{Fowler_2024,special-delivery-mailbox-types-2023} provide
type-theoretic guarantees for communication protocols. Our dependent type
families for message interfaces serve a related purpose---ensuring type-safe
communication---though we focus on the operational semantics of mailbox
management rather than protocol compliance.
Mailbox types~\cite{special-delivery-mailbox-types-2023} are particularly
relevant, as they enrich the type system to reason about mailbox contents,
complementing our approach of making the mailbox a first-class entity.

\paragraph{Event-Driven Systems}
The correct-by-construction approach of Event-B~\cite{dupont:tel-04165215}
inspires our guarded-action formulation. Our guards and actions parallel Event-B
events, with the effect system constraining the available operations. The
connection to Dijkstra's guarded command
language~\cite{dijkstra-guarded-commands} is also apparent in the non-overlapping
guard assumption.

\paragraph{Mailbox Decoupling}
The Kilim framework~\cite{kilim-framework} achieves mailbox decoupling through
specialised queues where actors pull messages when ready. However, Kilim treats
mailboxes as passive data structures. Actor selector
patterns~\cite{selectors-actors-2014} provide mechanisms for selective message
reception but do not promote the mailbox itself. Our work combines Kilim's
structural separation with the programmability of first-class actors, enabling
custom delivery policies, message prioritisation, and independent scaling of
message ingestion.

\paragraph{Formalisation of Actor Systems}
Talcott~\cite{Talcott1998} provides an actor theory in rewriting logic,
and Actario~\cite{actario} develops a Coq formalisation of the actor model.
Our dependent-type-theoretic approach offers a middle ground: more structured
than rewriting-based approaches, while remaining independent of a specific proof
assistant. The framework of~\cite{art-engines} provides the actor-like
semantics that we build upon, extending it with mailbox engines and
metatheoretic properties.

\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}

We have presented a formal framework for actor systems in which mailboxes are
promoted to independent, first-class actors---native actors with their own
configuration, behaviour, and lifecycle. Unlike prior approaches that treat
mailboxes as opaque primitives~\cite{formal-semantics-erlang} or passive data
structures~\cite{kilim-framework}, our mailbox actors are fully-fledged engines
whose guarded-action behaviours govern delivery, filtering, and scheduling.
The dependent-type-theoretic formulation, with all type families indexed by a
finite type of engine type indices, ensures type-safe communication by
construction.

The operational semantics cover engine creation with automatic mailbox pairing,
asynchronous message passing through mailbox intermediaries, behaviour
evaluation under well-formed behaviours with non-overlapping guards, effect
execution, and engine lifecycle management. We established type preservation,
progress, effect determinism, mailbox isolation, and eventual delivery as
metatheoretic properties.
The mailbox isolation property, in particular, captures the key design
invariant: all messages to a processing engine are mediated by its dedicated
mailbox actor, which is guaranteed by the structure of the transition rules.

\paragraph{Limitations}
The model assumes static engine types (no dynamic type extension), static
engine allocation (no migration between nodes), and fixed behaviours (no
runtime behaviour modification). The non-overlapping guard condition, bundled
structurally via well-formed behaviours, ensures determinism but restricts the
expressiveness of behaviours. Multiple
mailboxes per processing engine are excluded to avoid race conditions and
preserve delivery guarantees.

\paragraph{Future Work}
Several extensions are natural. First, a companion mechanisation in Lean~4
is under development; it includes a concrete instantiation of the abstract
framework for the causal delivery mailbox of \Cref{ex:causal-mailbox},
validating that the parametric definitions are usable in practice.
Second, a prototype implementation in Elixir---which provides a
robust actor runtime through the Erlang VM---would validate the practical
applicability. Third, dynamic engine type extension would enable open systems.
Finally, relaxing the non-overlapping guard assumption to permit prioritised or
nondeterministic guard selection would broaden the class of expressible
behaviours.
